payload.answer: Store only dataset-level metadata and compact summaries/representative samples for a 500-row repeated table; avoid storing all rows verbatim unless explicitly required for row-level retrieval.
payload.decision: Store structural metadata (columns, schema, and repeat pattern) plus a compact summary/statistics; do not store all rows verbatim unless explicitly requested. If needed, store only a small representative sample and an identifier/link back to the source dataset.

entity_resolution.reason: No specific real-world entities are provided beyond generic column/table structure; resolution to named people/places/organizations is not supported by the prompt.

payload.intent: Define how to store a large CSV table (500 rows) in a personal memory system to balance completeness with manageability.

memory_cards.statement: For large CSV inputs (e.g., ~500 rows) with repeated structures, store column schema and compact aggregates/summary rather than all rows verbatim, unless the user explicitly requests full row-level retention.

Prefer storing a representative sample (small) and summary metrics (e.g., counts, distinct values, min/max, frequent names/values) keyed by dataset/fixture identifiers to preserve utility without excessive memory usage.
The table appears to have repeating rows over columns {id, name, value}; without additional semantic requirements, treat individual rows as data points rather than standalone personal facts.

details: Applied a summarization-first storage policy appropriate for a large repeated CSV input.
Did not commit unverifiable row-level facts; preserved ambiguity about any semantic meaning of name/value pairs.

repair_options: If the user later requests row-level recall, switch to storing an index plus the full row set (or the relevant subset) with pagination/chunking.
If specific fields are needed for recall (e.g., certain 'name' values), store only rows matching those predicates plus summary of the remainder.
